result:
Sometimes, late at night, when the refrigerator stops humming and the traffic outside thins to an occasional sigh, I feel the apartment become a boat. Not a grand vessel—no masts, no salt-stung deckhands—but a crude raft of books, coffee cups, and half-broken promises, drifting on a dark tide I can’t name. The floorboards creak like hull planks, and I imagine a phosphorescent wake trailing behind us, proof that we’re moving even when the GPS of my life insists I’m stationary.  

I used to fight that drift. I drew schedules in Sharpie on the kitchen wall: 6:00 a.m. run, 6:45 gratitude journaling, 7:00 learn Mandarin, 7:30 revolutionize career. By 7:05 I was scrolling photos of strangers’ breakfasts, comparing their mango oat bowls to my sagging cereal, feeling like a failed start-up of one. The ink bled down the drywall in the humidity of my exhaled apologies. Eventually I painted over the whole calendar with leftover eggshell white. The wall looked relieved, the way a dog does when you finally drop the costume you’ve forced onto it.

Now I let the raft go where it likes. Some mornings it beaches itself at the corner bodega, where Carlos hands me coffee without asking—he sees the animal in my eyes that only speaks caffeine. Other nights we spin out past the city lights until the stars rearrange themselves into a constellation that looks suspiciously like my grandmother’s hands kneading dough. She died before I could learn the recipe, and the stars refuse to tell me whether she added an extra pinch of salt for sorrow or for love. I think maybe they’re the same seasoning.

I’ve been collecting obsolete things: a cassette of whale songs, a paper airline ticket jacket, the smell of rain on hot asphalt after the first drops evaporate but before the storm commits. They fit in a shoebox under the bed, a tiny museum of Almost. Curators think value lives in rarity; I think it lives in the moment just before disappearance. Like the second you notice the silence between lightning and thunder shrinking, telling you the story is heading your way faster than you planned.

Yesterday I found a child’s tooth in the pocket of my winter coat. It must have been placed there by my niece last December when I was drafted as a temporary Tooth Fairy—she specified the fairy should leave a poem instead of money, “because poems grow when you spend them.” I forgot, of course; the tooth got crushed into the seam by gloves and receipts, and the poem never materialized. I held that tiny enamel relic, feeling like I’d swallowed a compass that now points only to guilt. So I wrote the poem on the lint-covered tooth itself with a fineliner:  

“O pearl that once guarded a kingdom of soup and laughter,  
sleep now in the pocket dark.  
Dream of apples you will never bite,  
of whistles you will never shrill.  
When the coat is finally donated,  
may you ride the satin lining like a sled  
into the arms of someone who needs proof  
that small things used to live in big worlds.”  

I’m mailing the coat to goodwill tomorrow. The tooth stays in the box of Obsolete; it can keep the whale songs company, teach them how to bite time without teeth.

People talk about “starting over” as if life is a blackboard you can wipe. More often it’s a palimpsest: scratch off the top layer and you’ll find yesterday’s grocery list bleeding through, ghost-tomatoes haunting today’s seminar notes. I’ve stopped trying to erase. Instead I layer, like those Buddhist prayer flags that disintegrate so the wind can read them. My mistakes get thin enough for sunlight, and sometimes that’s the only forgiveness required.

At 3:12 a.m. the city grid flickers, a momentary brownout that makes every alarm clock forget its digits. For three heartbeats we’re all time-free. I stand on the fire escape in dinosaur socks and feel the vertical neighborhood synchronize its breath: the insomniac novelist upstairs exhales, the newborn next door inhales, the couple across the courtyard pause mid-fight to notice the dark. It’s the closest thing to communion I’ve found outside a song. Then the LEDs flood back, cruelly bright, and everyone remembers who owes whom an apology. Still, those three seconds are banked somewhere inside me, accruing interest.

I keep thinking I’ll run out of words, that the dictionary will send a final notice: “Dear Madam, you have reached your quota of syllables. Please return ‘hiraeth’ and ‘petrichor’ at your earliest convenience.” But the nouns multiply like socks that vanish from the dryer and reappear as extra tupperware lids. Language is a fungus; darkness is its favorite mulch. The more I feed it disappointment, the brighter it fruits into impossible colors. Yesterday I discovered the color “aftersmoke,” the hue left in the air when the barbecue ends but the summer hasn’t yet admitted defeat. Try to paint it and you’ll only get the grey of burnout; name it and you can taste the neighbor’s laughter drifting over the fence like a question mark.

My father used to say we’re all walking each other home. He stole the line from Ram Dass, but he gave it a Midwestern accent, which somehow made it sound like permission rather than philosophy. When the Alzheimer’s took him he forgot the sentence halfway through, left us stranded on the sidewalk with a map missing its final block. I finished it for him in the hospice parking lot, whispering to the steering wheel: “…even when we don’t recognize the houses anymore.” The car smelled of orange peels and antiseptic; I cried like a windshield wiper that’s lost its rhythm, swipe-screech, long pause, swipe again.

Tonight the raft feels especially buoyant, as if the ocean below has been replaced by the collective breath of everyone I’ve ever disappointed. That sounds cruel, but it isn’t—breath is warm, and it rises. I lie on my back in the living-room-cum-cabin, press my palm to the pine-scented nothing of cheap Ikea floor, and feel the upwelling. We’re lifted, gently, toward a ceiling I painted with glow-in-the-dark constellations during another life. They’ve faded; only half a star remains, a phosphorescent comma that refuses to end the sentence. Good enough. I navigate by partial punctuation, by the smell of burnt toast that somehow still comforts, by the sound of the upstairs tenant practicing saxophone at 2 a.m.—each missed note a breadcrumb leading further into the forest of being alive.

Tomorrow I’ll probably crawl back into calendars and passwords. But right now the raft accepts my weight without ledger. The night tide turns, carrying us past the breakwater of streetlights, into the open page where clocks have no claws. I close my eyes and listen for the hush between heartbeats—that tiny doorway where the poem I owe the universe is already writing itself in the dark.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
